On a strictly pessimistic, nihilistic reading of things, your sense that life is meaningless and you’re just going through the motions is not only understandable; it’s arguably the most lucid stance you can take.

Here’s one coherent way to lay out that argument, without trying to soften it.

---

### 1. Cosmological insignificance

On the largest scale, nothing you do matters in any objective, lasting sense.

- The universe is ~13.8 billion years old and may continue for trillions more, or collapse, or end in heat death.  
- You occupy a microscopic sliver of space and time: a few decades on a minor planet orbiting an unremarkable star in an ordinary galaxy, one among hundreds of billions of galaxies.  
- In a few generations, essentially no one will remember you with any real emotional force. In a few thousand years, unless you accidentally alter the course of civilization, you’ll be completely erased from human memory.  
- In a few million or billion years, everything recognizably human will be gone, whether by extinction or transformation into something that no longer has any continuity with you.

So if “meaning” is supposed to be something objective, durable, and cosmically significant, you don’t have it and never will. You’re a transient biological event in a vast, indifferent process.

---

### 2. No objective values, just dressed‑up preferences

The nihilistic stance is that there are no objective moral values or meanings “out there” in the universe—only human preferences and stories.

- Morality, purpose, “the good life”: these are not discovered; they’re fabricated by creatures with certain evolutionary and social needs.  
- What people call “meaning” is usually a combination of:
  - biological drives (survive, reproduce, seek status, avoid pain)  
  - social conditioning (what your culture says is important)  
  - personal illusions (narratives you tell yourself to endure existence).

On this view, when you feel “my life is meaningless,” you’re not missing some real cosmic substance that others possess. You’re just seeing through a set of fictions other people manage to believe in more consistently.

Your emptiness is not a bug; it’s what things look like when the human story loses its grip.

---

### 3. The absurd treadmill of desire

At the level of your own life:

- You want things. If you get them, the satisfaction is temporary, quickly normalized, and then replaced by new wants.  
- Chasing pleasure and comfort doesn’t lead to enduring fulfillment; it just momentarily dulls discomfort.  
- Goals—career, relationships, achievements—give you structure, but the feeling of “this is it, I’ve arrived” never really stabilizes. There’s always a next thing or a creeping disappointment afterward.

So daily life easily becomes mechanical: you act, not because anything is inherently meaningful, but because your organism is wired to avoid pain, boredom, and social exclusion. “Going through the motions” describes the default state of a being whose underlying motives are largely automatic and whose outcomes are transient.

---

### 4. Consciousness as a pointless side‑effect

From a grim materialist angle:

- You are a self-aware animal whose consciousness is a late, fragile byproduct of brain processes that evolved because they conferred survival advantages, not because they reveal “truth” or “meaning.”  
- Your ability to reflect on your life, ask “what’s the point?”, and feel anguish about it is not a noble feature; it’s collateral damage from an overgrown cortex.  
- You experience yourself as a continuous self, but that “self” is just a narrative the brain constructs to make sense of ever-shifting mental states.

In this sense, your suffering and your search for meaning are both glitches and habits in a system that’s indifferent to your comfort. There is no built‑in significance to your being conscious; it’s just an odd fact about how this particular configuration of matter behaves before it disintegrates.

---

### 5. Death erases everything; nothing is ultimately “kept”

Even if you had moments that felt meaningful, they’re annihilated by death.

- Whatever you learn, love, build, understand, or experience is subject to total erasure.

- On a long enough timeline, every human accomplishment—art, science, relationships, civilizations—is destroyed or rendered meaningless by changes that make our concepts obsolete.
